author: Irenaeus of Lyon
interpretation: Irenaeus reads Genesis 3:15 as the first messianic prophecy — the protoevangelium. The 'seed of the woman' who will crush the serpent's head is Christ, and Mary is the new Eve whose obedience reverses Eve's disobedience. This verse establishes the trajectory of the entire redemptive narrative from the moment of the Fall.
context: Irenaeus's Eve-Mary typology became foundational for both Eastern and Western Mariology. His reading of Genesis 3:15 as messianic was virtually unanimous in the early church.

author: Augustine of Hippo
interpretation: Augustine sees the enmity between the woman's seed and the serpent as a perpetual spiritual conflict between the City of God and the City of Man. The 'crushing' is ultimately accomplished through Christ but plays out through the ongoing struggle of the church against the devil across all of history.
context: Augustine's two-cities framework, rooted partly in Genesis 3:15, became the dominant philosophy of history in Western Christianity through the medieval period.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas treats Genesis 3:15 as a prophecy with both immediate and ultimate fulfillment. In the proximate sense, it describes the natural human revulsion toward serpents. In its fullest sense, the 'seed' is Christ and the crushing of the head signifies the definitive defeat of Satan through the cross and resurrection.
context: Aquinas's layered reading (literal and spiritual senses) exemplifies the medieval fourfold interpretive method, where a single verse can operate on multiple levels simultaneously.

author: Martin Luther
interpretation: Luther calls Genesis 3:15 the first gospel preached on earth. In the very moment of pronouncing judgment, God embeds a promise of deliverance. Luther sees this as paradigmatic for how God always works — law and gospel together, judgment and mercy inseparable. Adam and Eve received this promise by faith and were justified, making them the first believers.
context: Luther's reading of Genesis 3:15 as the first proclamation of the gospel reflects his central hermeneutical principle: all of Scripture ultimately testifies to Christ and the promise of salvation by faith.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin is more cautious than Luther. He acknowledges the messianic reading but insists the primary sense is collective — the ongoing war between humanity and evil. The 'seed' refers first to all of Eve's descendants who resist the serpent, though this conflict finds its ultimate resolution in Christ. Calvin resists over-reading a single verse when the fuller revelation comes later.
context: Calvin's restraint here illustrates his exegetical method: let each text say what it says in its own context before importing later theological categories. This made his commentaries influential among critical scholars despite his conservative theology.

author: Hermann Gunkel
interpretation: Gunkel reads Genesis 3:15 as an etiological myth explaining the natural enmity between humans and snakes — nothing more. The verse describes why humans instinctively strike at serpents and serpents bite at human heels. Messianic readings are later theological impositions on an originally simple folk tradition.
context: Gunkel pioneered form criticism and classified Genesis narratives by genre (myth, saga, legend). His demythologizing reading of 3:15 directly challenged centuries of messianic interpretation and remains influential in academic scholarship.

author: G.K. Beale
interpretation: Beale traces a 'seed theology' through the entire Bible originating from Genesis 3:15. The line of the faithful seed — Abel, Seth, Noah, Abraham, David — is continually threatened by the seed of the serpent. This conflict structures biblical history and reaches its climax in Revelation 12, where the dragon pursues the woman's child. Genesis 3:15 is the DNA of the biblical plotline.
context: Beale's 'seed of the woman' theme has become a widely used framework in biblical theology courses and whole-Bible narrative approaches to Scripture.
